old_claim.note: Protein Engineering is modeled as a necessary component or method for this technology in the current graph.

new_claim.note: Protein engineering supports Cas9 domain characterization and later optimization, but the 2012 programmable nuclease platform is primarily RNA-guided rather than dependent on engineered Cas9 variants.

source_shape.source_locator: Abstract and Figures 2 and 5 describing nuclease-domain mutants and single engineered guide RNA programming of Cas9.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The paper shows Cas9 is programmed by guide RNA and uses nuclease-domain mutants to characterize cleavage domains, while the programmable platform itself is based on RNA-directed Cas9 activity.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The citation licenses an enabling-method edge because protein modifications are used for domain analysis and optimization context, while the required dependency is RNA-guided Cas9 function rather than engineered Cas9 protein variants.

ontology_before: The edge claimed protein engineering was a hard component or method prerequisite for programmable Cas9 nuclease.
ontology_after: The edge only claims protein engineering enables characterization and optimization of Cas9; the core platform is modeled through CRISPR adaptive immunity, molecular biology, and RNA-guided Cas9 activity.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Invariant changed deliberately: the graph no longer treats engineered protein variants as required for the original programmable Cas9 nuclease platform.
would_reject_if: The source showed the 2012 programmable Cas9 platform required engineered Cas9 variants rather than RNA guidance.
The edge remained required without a direct source showing protein engineering as a hard prerequisite.
The new rationale failed to distinguish domain-characterization methods from the core programmable-nuclease mechanism.
short_reason: Protein engineering is relevant to Cas9 analysis and later optimization, but the source supports RNA-guided programmability as the core platform mechanism.
